title: A Physics Integrator, Gravity & a Side-Scrolling Platformer
html:
What makes a platformer feel good

Platformers live or die on movement feel. The difference between a sloppy, floaty character and a tight, responsive one comes down to a small number of numbers: gravity, jump velocity, acceleration, friction, and how collisions resolve. In this lesson you will write a semi-implicit Euler integrator, add gravity and jumping, and resolve collisions against a tile grid axis-by-axis so the player never tunnels through floors.

Semi-implicit Euler

The naive integrator updates position with the old velocity. Semi-implicit (also called symplectic) Euler updates velocity first, then uses the new velocity for position. It is one line different and dramatically more stable for gravity-driven motion.

integrate
// semi-implicit Euler: update velocity, THEN position vel.y += GRAVITY * dt; // gravity in px/s^2, e.g. 1800 vel.y = fminf(vel.y, MAX_FALL); // terminal velocity clamp pos.x += vel.x * dt; pos.y += vel.y * dt;

Run gravity at something like 1800 px/s² and a jump impulse around -650 px/s and you get a snappy arc. Tune these by feel, not by realism.

Horizontal movement with acceleration and friction

Instant velocity changes feel robotic. Accelerate toward a target speed and decelerate with friction when there is no input.

horizontal.cpp
float target = 0.0f; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_LEFT)) target -= RUN_SPEED; // e.g. 220 if (IsKeyDown(KEY_RIGHT)) target += RUN_SPEED; float accel = (target != 0.0f) ? GROUND_ACCEL : GROUND_FRICTION; vel.x = Lerp(vel.x, target, 1.0f - expf(-accel * dt));
Tip.
1.0f - expf(-k*dt) is a framerate-independent smoothing factor. It approaches the target at the same real-world rate no matter the framerate, unlike a raw vel += (target-vel)*0.2f.

Swept tile collision, one axis at a time

The robust trick for tile platformers: move on X, resolve X penetrations; then move on Y, resolve Y penetrations. Resolving each axis separately avoids the classic corner-snag bug where you get stuck on flat floors.

collide.cpp
// tiles[] is row-major; solid() tests a tile coord bool solid(int tx, int ty); void resolveAxis(Rectangle& box, float& vAxis, bool horizontal, bool& onGround) { int x0 = (int)(box.x / TILE); int x1 = (int)((box.x + box.width - 1) / TILE); int y0 = (int)(box.y / TILE); int y1 = (int)((box.y + box.height - 1) / TILE); for (int ty = y0; ty <= y1; ty++) for (int tx = x0; tx <= x1; tx++) { if (!solid(tx, ty)) continue; Rectangle t = { tx*(float)TILE, ty*(float)TILE, TILE, TILE }; if (!CheckCollisionRecs(box, t)) continue; if (horizontal) { if (vAxis > 0) box.x = t.x - box.width; else box.x = t.x + TILE; } else { if (vAxis > 0) { box.y = t.y - box.height; onGround = true; } else { box.y = t.y + TILE; } } vAxis = 0.0f; } }

The caller moves the box, then resolves. Note we set onGround only when we hit a floor moving downward — that flag gates jumping.

step.cpp
bool onGround = false; box.x += vel.x * dt; resolveAxis(box, vel.x, true, onGround); box.y += vel.y * dt; resolveAxis(box, vel.y, false, onGround); if (onGround && IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE)) vel.y = JUMP_VEL; // -650

Game-feel polish: coyote time and jump buffering

Two cheap tricks make jumping forgiving. Coyote time lets the player jump for ~80ms after walking off a ledge. Jump buffering remembers a jump press for ~120ms so a slightly-early press still fires on landing.

feel.cpp
if (onGround) coyote = COYOTE_TIME; else coyote -= dt; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE)) buffer = BUFFER_TIME; else buffer -= dt; if (buffer > 0 && coyote > 0) { vel.y = JUMP_VEL; buffer = 0; coyote = 0; }
Law of platformers.
Resolve collisions per-axis, integrate with semi-implicit Euler, and add coyote/buffer time. These three habits separate amateur and professional movement.
🎮 Build This: Platformer — Leap

This STARTS a new genre: the side-scrolling platformer. It is the foundation the next two lessons (tilemaps/camera, then Metroidvania) extend.

  1. Define a tile grid (e.g. 30×17 of TILE=32) with a string-art level: '#' solid, '.' empty.
  2. Make a player struct with pos, vel, an AABB box, and an onGround flag.
  3. Each fixed step: read input, apply horizontal accel/friction, apply gravity, then move-and-resolve per axis.
  4. Add jumping gated on onGround, then layer in coyote time and jump buffering.
  5. Draw solid tiles and the player rectangle; print vel on screen and tune gravity/jump until the arc feels right.
  6. Add a hazard tile that resets the player to a spawn point on contact.

title: Tilemaps, Layers & a 2D Camera
html:
From one screen to a whole world

The platformer from the last lesson fit on one screen. Real levels scroll. In this lesson you will build a proper layered tilemap with culling so you only draw what is visible, then drive a Camera2D that follows the player smoothly, stays inside the world bounds, and renders background layers with parallax for depth.

A data-driven tilemap

Store the map as flat arrays — one per layer — so cache access is linear and serialization is trivial. A layer is just width × height integers indexing into a tileset.

tilemap.h
struct Layer { std::vector<int> cells; // -1 = empty float parallax = 1.0f; // 1 = moves with world, 0.5 = half speed }; struct Tilemap { int w, h, tile; std::vector<Layer> layers; Texture2D tileset; int cols; // tiles per row in the atlas int at(const Layer& L, int x, int y) const { return L.cells[y*w + x]; } };

Culling: only draw what the camera sees

Drawing a 500×200 map every frame is wasteful. Compute the visible tile range from the camera and loop only over that window. With a Camera2D you can derive the visible world rectangle from the screen corners.

draw.cpp
void drawLayer(const Tilemap& m, const Layer& L, Camera2D cam) { // world-space corners of the screen Vector2 tl = GetScreenToWorld2D({0,0}, cam); Vector2 br = GetScreenToWorld2D({(float)GetScreenWidth(),(float)GetScreenHeight()}, cam); int x0 = fmaxf(0, (int)(tl.x / m.tile)); int y0 = fmaxf(0, (int)(tl.y / m.tile)); int x1 = fminf(m.w - 1, (int)(br.x / m.tile)); int y1 = fminf(m.h - 1, (int)(br.y / m.tile)); for (int y = y0; y <= y1; y++) for (int x = x0; x <= x1; x++) { int id = m.at(L, x, y); if (id < 0) continue; Rectangle src = { (float)(id % m.cols)*m.tile, (float)(id / m.cols)*m.tile, (float)m.tile, (float)m.tile }; DrawTextureRec(m.tileset, src, { (float)(x*m.tile), (float)(y*m.tile) }, WHITE); } }
Tip.
Always cull. A culled tilemap renders in constant time regardless of map size — the cost scales with screen area, not world area.

Camera2D: follow with smoothing and bounds

raylib's Camera2D has a target (the world point at the camera center), an offset (where that target lands on screen), zoom, and rotation. Set offset to the screen center and lerp target toward the player.

camera.cpp
Camera2D cam = {0}; cam.offset = { sw/2.0f, sh/2.0f }; cam.zoom = 1.0f; // each frame: smooth follow Vector2 goal = player.center(); cam.target.x = Lerp(cam.target.x, goal.x, 1.0f - expf(-8.0f * dt)); cam.target.y = Lerp(cam.target.y, goal.y, 1.0f - expf(-8.0f * dt)); // clamp so we never show outside the world float halfW = (sw/2.0f) / cam.zoom, halfH = (sh/2.0f) / cam.zoom; cam.target.x = Clamp(cam.target.x, halfW, m.w*m.tile - halfW); cam.target.y = Clamp(cam.target.y, halfH, m.h*m.tile - halfH);

Parallax layers

Distant backgrounds should move slower than the foreground. Render each background layer through a camera whose target is scaled by that layer's parallax factor.

parallax.cpp
for (auto& L : m.layers) { Camera2D lc = cam; lc.target = { cam.target.x * L.parallax, cam.target.y * L.parallax }; BeginMode2D(lc); drawLayer(m, L, lc); EndMode2D(); }
Lab.
Add a deadzone: only move the camera when the player leaves a small box around the center. This is how Mario keeps the camera still during small hops but scrolls during runs.
Law of cameras.
Render inside BeginMode2D/EndMode2D, follow with exponential smoothing, and clamp the target to world bounds so the player never sees the void.
🎮 Build This: World Tech — Scrolling Platformer World

This EXTENDS the platformer (Leap) from p4-9. Same physics and collision; now the world is large and scrolls.

  1. Convert the single-screen map into a wide Tilemap with at least three layers: far background (parallax 0.3), midground (0.6), and the solid play layer (1.0).
  2. Move all collision logic to read the solid layer's cells; keep per-axis resolution from p4-9.
  3. Add a Camera2D that smoothly follows the player and clamps to world bounds.
  4. Implement culled drawing so only on-screen tiles render; verify by printing the tile count drawn.
  5. Render background layers with parallax, then the play layer, then the player inside BeginMode2D.
  6. Add a deadzone so small jumps do not jitter the camera.

title: Building a Metroidvania: Interconnected Rooms & Abilities
html:
A Metroidvania is a graph of rooms

A Metroidvania is a platformer whose world is a connected graph of rooms, where progression is ability-gated: a door or gap is impassable until you unlock the right power. In this lesson you combine the tilemap/camera tech from p4-10 with the scene stack from p4-8 to build interconnected rooms with doors and unlockable abilities like the double jump and the dash.

Rooms and the connection model

Each room is a tilemap plus a list of door triggers. A door knows the target room id and the spawn point inside it. When the player overlaps a door, you load the new room and place the player at the matching entrance.

room.h
struct Door { Rectangle trigger; // world-space zone in this room int targetRoom; Vector2 spawn; // where player appears in target int requiredAbility; // -1 = always open }; struct Room { Tilemap map; std::vector<Door> doors; std::vector<Pickup> pickups; };

Abilities as a bitmask

Track unlocked powers in a single integer with one bit per ability. This makes gating checks trivial and saving a one-liner.

abilities.h
enum Ability { ABIL_DOUBLEJUMP = 1<<0, ABIL_DASH = 1<<1, ABIL_WALLCLING = 1<<2 }; unsigned unlocked = 0; inline bool has(Ability a) { return (unlocked & a) != 0; } inline void grant(Ability a) { unlocked |= a; }

Gating movement on abilities

The double jump only fires if you have already used your ground jump and you own the ability. The dash is a short burst with a cooldown, also gated.

powers.cpp
if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE)) { if (onGround || coyote > 0) { vel.y = JUMP_VEL; jumpsLeft = 1; } else if (jumpsLeft > 0 && has(ABIL_DOUBLEJUMP)) { vel.y = JUMP_VEL; jumpsLeft--; } } if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_LEFT_SHIFT) && has(ABIL_DASH) && dashCd <= 0) { vel.x = facing * DASH_SPEED; // e.g. 700 dashTime = 0.15f; dashCd = 0.6f; } if (dashTime > 0) { dashTime -= dt; vel.y = 0; } // no gravity during dash dashCd -= dt;
Tip.
Reset jumpsLeft to 1 whenever onGround becomes true. The double jump gives a second, not infinite jumps.

Room transitions through the scene stack

Reuse the scene stack from p4-8. The exploration scene owns the current room index. When the player enters a door whose requiredAbility is satisfied, swap rooms and reposition.

transition.cpp
for (const Door& d : rooms[cur].doors) { if (!CheckCollisionRecs(player.box, d.trigger)) continue; if (d.requiredAbility >= 0 && !has((Ability)d.requiredAbility)) { hint = "Locked: need an ability"; continue; } cur = d.targetRoom; player.setPos(d.spawn); camSnap = true; // hard-cut camera on room change break; }

Pickups that unlock the world

An ability pickup grants its bit on contact, then triggers a celebratory pause. After grabbing the dash, the player can suddenly cross gaps that were impassable — that aha moment is the genre's whole design language.

pickup.cpp
for (auto& p : rooms[cur].pickups) { if (p.taken) continue; if (CheckCollisionRecs(player.box, p.box)) { grant(p.ability); p.taken = true; banner = p.name; bannerTime = 2.0f; } }
Law of Metroidvanias.
Design backward from abilities. Place each locked path before the player can possibly own its key, so the world quietly teaches them where to return.
🎮 Build This: Metroidvania — Vault

This STARTS a new genre: the Metroidvania. It directly EXTENDS the scrolling platformer (p4-10 tech) and the scene stack (p4-8).

  1. Build at least four rooms as separate tilemaps; give each a list of doors linking to other rooms with spawn points.
  2. Store unlocked abilities in a bitmask; implement double jump and dash gated on it.
  3. Place a dash pickup behind a wide gap that only the dash can cross, and a double-jump pickup behind a tall wall.
  4. Add a locked door requiring an ability; show a hint when the player cannot pass.
  5. On room entry, reposition the player and hard-cut the camera, then resume smoothing.
  6. Show an ability-unlocked banner, then verify the world opens up as powers are gained.

title: Top-Down Adventure: A Zelda-Like World
html:
Switching to a top-down world

No gravity here — the player moves freely in eight directions across a top-down world of screen-sized rooms. In this lesson you build a Zelda-like with 8-directional movement, room-to-room transitions that slide the camera, simple enemies, pickups, and a heart-based combat system with a sword swing and invincibility frames.

Eight-directional movement

Read both axes, build a direction vector, and normalize it so diagonal movement is not faster than cardinal movement — a classic beginner bug.

move.cpp
Vector2 dir = {0,0}; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_W)) dir.y -= 1; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_S)) dir.y += 1; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_A)) dir.x -= 1; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_D)) dir.x += 1; if (dir.x != 0 || dir.y != 0) { dir = Vector2Normalize(dir); facing = dir; // remember last facing for the sword } player.pos = Vector2Add(player.pos, Vector2Scale(dir, SPEED * dt));

Collision against solid tiles reuses the per-axis resolution idea from p4-9, just without gravity. Move X, resolve; move Y, resolve.

Room transitions that slide

In top-down adventures the camera does not follow continuously — it snaps room by room. When the player walks past an edge, slide the camera one screen over and shift the player to the opposite edge.

rooms.cpp
// world is a grid of rooms; each room is SW x SH pixels if (player.pos.x > (roomX+1)*SW && roomX+1 < worldCols) { roomX++; transitioning = true; slideFrom = camTarget; slideTo = { roomX*SW + SW/2.0f, camTarget.y }; player.pos.x += PLAYER_NUDGE; // push past the edge } // during a transition, lerp the camera and freeze gameplay if (transitioning) { slideT += dt / 0.4f; camTarget = Vector2Lerp(slideFrom, slideTo, fminf(slideT, 1.0f)); if (slideT >= 1.0f) { transitioning = false; slideT = 0; } }
Tip.
Freeze enemy AI and the player during the slide. Letting things move mid-transition feels chaotic and lets enemies hit a player who cannot react.

Hearts, damage, and i-frames

Health is measured in half-hearts. When an enemy touches the player, deal damage, knock the player back, and grant a short window of invincibility frames so a single contact does not drain the whole health bar.

combat.cpp
int hp = 6; // 3 full hearts, 2 halves each float iframes = 0; void hurt(int amount, Vector2 from) { if (iframes > 0) return; hp -= amount; iframes = 1.0f; knockback = Vector2Scale(Vector2Normalize(Vector2Subtract(player.pos, from)), 260.0f); } // each frame iframes -= dt; player.pos = Vector2Add(player.pos, Vector2Scale(knockback, dt)); knockback = Vector2Scale(knockback, expf(-10.0f * dt)); // decay

Render the player blinking while iframes > 0 by skipping the draw on alternating frames — instant, readable feedback.

The sword swing

A swing spawns a short-lived hitbox in front of the player based on facing. Any enemy overlapping it during the active window takes damage.

sword.cpp
if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_J) && swingTime <= 0) swingTime = 0.2f; if (swingTime > 0) { swingTime -= dt; Vector2 c = Vector2Add(player.center(), Vector2Scale(facing, 22.0f)); Rectangle hit = { c.x-14, c.y-14, 28, 28 }; for (auto& e : enemies) if (e.alive && CheckCollisionRecs(hit, e.box)) { e.hp--; e.flash = 0.1f; } }
Law of top-down combat.
Knockback plus i-frames plus a visible hit-flash equals readable, fair fighting. Without all three, hits feel either spongy or unfair.
🎮 Build This: Top-Down Adventure — Greenwood

This STARTS a new genre: the top-down Zelda-like. It reuses per-axis collision (p4-9) and tilemaps (p4-10) but introduces gravity-free, room-snapped movement.

  1. Build a small world grid of screen-sized rooms, each a tilemap with solid walls.
  2. Implement normalized 8-directional movement with per-axis tile collision.
  3. Add edge-triggered room transitions that slide the camera and freeze gameplay.
  4. Place a few wandering enemies that damage the player on contact, with knockback and i-frames.
  5. Add a sword swing that spawns a directional hitbox; make enemies flash and die.
  6. Draw a heart UI from hp, a key pickup, and a locked door that opens once the key is held.

title: Twin-Stick Shooter & a Particle System
html:
Aim-independent movement

A twin-stick shooter decouples movement from aiming: one stick (or WASD) moves, the other (or the mouse) aims and fires. In this lesson you build that control scheme, a bullet pool so firing never allocates, and a reusable particle system for muzzle flashes, trails, and explosions.

Move and aim separately

Movement is a normalized direction vector. Aim is a separate angle derived from the mouse or right stick. The player sprite rotates to face the aim, not the movement.

controls.cpp
Vector2 mv = {0,0}; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_W)) mv.y--; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_S)) mv.y++; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_A)) mv.x--; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_D)) mv.x++; if (mv.x||mv.y) mv = Vector2Normalize(mv); player.pos = Vector2Add(player.pos, Vector2Scale(mv, MOVE_SPEED*dt)); // aim toward the mouse (in world space) Vector2 aim = Vector2Subtract(GetScreenToWorld2D(GetMousePosition(), cam), player.pos); float aimAngle = atan2f(aim.y, aim.x);

A bullet pool

Spawning bullets with new on every shot fragments memory and stutters. Instead, preallocate a fixed array and recycle slots. Each bullet has an alive flag; firing finds a dead slot, killing happens on hit or timeout.

bullets.cpp
struct Bullet { Vector2 pos, vel; float life; bool alive; }; static Bullet pool[512]; void fire(Vector2 from, float ang) { for (Bullet& b : pool) if (!b.alive) { b.alive = true; b.pos = from; b.life = 1.2f; b.vel = { cosf(ang)*BULLET_SPEED, sinf(ang)*BULLET_SPEED }; return; } } void updateBullets(float dt) { for (Bullet& b : pool) { if (!b.alive) continue; b.pos = Vector2Add(b.pos, Vector2Scale(b.vel, dt)); if ((b.life -= dt) <= 0) b.alive = false; } }
Tip.
A fire-rate cooldown prevents one shot per frame. Track fireCd -= dt and only fire when it reaches zero, then reset it to 1.0f / ROUNDS_PER_SEC.

A reusable particle system

Particles are the secret sauce of game feel. Build one generic pool with position, velocity, lifetime, color, and size. Emitters just push particles with different parameters — sparks, smoke, trails all share the same update and draw.

particles.cpp
struct Particle { Vector2 pos, vel; float life, maxLife, size; Color col; bool alive; }; static Particle parts[2048]; void emit(Vector2 p, int n, float speed, Color c) { for (int i=0; i<n; i++) for (Particle& q : parts) if (!q.alive) { float a = GetRandomValue(0,628)/100.0f; float s = speed * (GetRandomValue(40,100)/100.0f); q = { p, { cosf(a)*s, sinf(a)*s }, 0.6f, 0.6f, 4.0f, c, true }; break; } } void updateParticles(float dt) { for (Particle& q : parts) { if (!q.alive) continue; q.pos = Vector2Add(q.pos, Vector2Scale(q.vel, dt)); q.vel = Vector2Scale(q.vel, expf(-3.0f*dt)); // drag if ((q.life -= dt) <= 0) q.alive = false; } } void drawParticles() { for (Particle& q : parts) if (q.alive) { float t = q.life / q.maxLife; Color c = Fade(q.col, t); // fade out over life DrawCircleV(q.pos, q.size * t, c); } }

Now wire feedback: call emit with a few sparks at the muzzle on each shot, a burst of orange on enemy death, and a thin trail behind each bullet by emitting one fading particle per frame at the bullet's position.

Law of game feel.
Pools over allocation, fade over hard cuts, and particles on every impact. Cheap effects, layered, are what make a shooter feel alive.
🎮 Build This: Twin-Stick Shooter — Overload

This STARTS a new genre: the twin-stick / top-down shooter. It reuses normalized movement (p4-12) and introduces pooling and particles used by later genres.

  1. Implement WASD movement and mouse aiming so the player faces the cursor while moving any direction.
  2. Add a bullet pool of 512 with a fire-rate cooldown; fire from the muzzle along the aim angle.
  3. Spawn enemies that steer toward the player; kill them when a bullet overlaps and recycle the bullet.
  4. Build the generic particle pool with emit, update, and draw.
  5. Emit muzzle sparks on fire, an explosion burst on enemy death, and a fading trail behind each bullet.
  6. Add a score counter and waves of increasing enemy count; verify zero allocations during gameplay.

title: Raycasting: A Wolfenstein-Style First-Person Shooter
html:
Faking 3D from a 2D map

Wolfenstein 3D rendered a convincing 3D world without any real 3D, using raycasting: for each vertical column of the screen, cast one ray across a 2D grid, find the first wall it hits, and draw a vertical slice whose height is inversely proportional to the distance. In this lesson you implement a DDA (Digital Differential Analysis) raycaster with textured walls, depth shading, and billboard sprites for enemies.

The player and the map

The map is a 2D grid of integers — zero is empty, nonzero is a wall texture id. The player has a position, a direction vector, and a camera plane perpendicular to direction that controls the field of view.

player.cpp
Vector2 pos = { 3.5f, 3.5f }; // in map cells Vector2 dir = { -1, 0 }; // facing Vector2 plane = { 0, 0.66f }; // ~66 deg FOV // rotate both dir and plane to turn void rotate(float a) { float c = cosf(a), s = sinf(a); dir = { dir.x*c - dir.y*s, dir.x*s + dir.y*c }; plane = { plane.x*c - plane.y*s, plane.x*s + plane.y*c }; }

DDA: stepping cell by cell

For each screen column x, compute the ray direction, then walk the grid using DDA — always stepping to the nearest grid line. This is exact and never misses a wall, unlike fixed-step sampling.

cast.cpp
for (int x = 0; x < W; x++) { float camX = 2.0f * x / W - 1.0f; // -1..1 across screen Vector2 rd = { dir.x + plane.x*camX, dir.y + plane.y*camX }; int mx = (int)pos.x, my = (int)pos.y; float ddx = fabsf(1.0f/rd.x), ddy = fabsf(1.0f/rd.y); int stepX = rd.x < 0 ? -1 : 1, stepY = rd.y < 0 ? -1 : 1; float sdx = rd.x < 0 ? (pos.x-mx)*ddx : (mx+1-pos.x)*ddx; float sdy = rd.y < 0 ? (pos.y-my)*ddy : (my+1-pos.y)*ddy; int side = 0; while (true) { if (sdx < sdy) { sdx += ddx; mx += stepX; side = 0; } else { sdy += ddy; my += stepY; side = 1; } if (map[my][mx] > 0) break; } // perpendicular distance avoids fisheye distortion float dist = side == 0 ? (sdx - ddx) : (sdy - ddy);

Projecting the wall slice

Wall height on screen is H / dist. Center it vertically. The perpendicular distance (not the Euclidean ray length) is what prevents the fisheye warp at screen edges.

slice.cpp
int lineH = (int)(H / dist); int y0 = -lineH/2 + H/2; if (y0 < 0) y0 = 0; int y1 = lineH/2 + H/2; if (y1 >= H) y1 = H-1; // where along the wall did we hit? gives the texture column float wallX = side==0 ? pos.y + dist*rd.y : pos.x + dist*rd.x; wallX -= floorf(wallX); int texX = (int)(wallX * TEX); // depth shade: darker far away, and y-sides darker than x-sides float shade = Clamp(1.2f - dist*0.08f, 0.2f, 1.0f); if (side == 1) shade *= 0.7f; Rectangle src = { (float)texX, 0, 1, TEX }; Rectangle dst = { (float)x, (float)y0, 1, (float)(y1-y0) }; DrawTexturePro(walls, src, dst, {0,0}, 0, Fade(WHITE, shade)); zbuffer[x] = dist; // store per-column depth for sprites }
Tip.
Store the per-column wall distance in a zbuffer. Sprites use it to hide behind walls — a sprite pixel only draws if it is closer than the wall in that column.

Billboard sprites with depth

Enemies and items are flat billboards. Transform each into camera space with the inverse of the [plane | dir] matrix, then draw it as a column-by-column strip, skipping columns where the zbuffer says a wall is closer.

sprite.cpp
float rx = sp.x - pos.x, ry = sp.y - pos.y; float inv = 1.0f / (plane.x*dir.y - dir.x*plane.y); float tx = inv * (dir.y*rx - dir.x*ry); float ty = inv * (-plane.y*rx + plane.x*ry); // depth (must be > 0) if (ty <= 0) return; // behind the camera int screenX = (int)((W/2) * (1 + tx/ty)); int size = (int)(H / ty); // for each column of the sprite, draw only if ty < zbuffer[col]
Watch out.
Always sort sprites far-to-near before drawing, and surround the map with solid walls so the DDA loop can never step out of bounds and crash.
🎮 Build This: FPS (Raycasting) — Catacomb

This STARTS a new genre: the raycast first-person shooter, Wolfenstein-style. No prior game extends into it, but it reuses the particle pool (p4-13) for hit effects.

  1. Define a grid map with bordered walls and several wall texture ids.
  2. Implement player movement and turning that rotates dir and plane together, with wall collision.
  3. Cast one DDA ray per column, compute perpendicular distance, and draw textured wall slices with depth shading.
  4. Fill the zbuffer per column so sprites can occlude correctly.
  5. Add billboard enemy sprites, sorted by distance and clipped against the zbuffer.
  6. Add a hitscan gun: cast a center ray, damage the nearest enemy in front, and emit particles on hit.

title: Pseudo-3D Rendering: An OutRun-Style Racing Game
html:
A road made of segments

OutRun-style racers fake 3D with a different trick than raycasting: the road is a long list of segments, each a slice of road at a Z depth, and you project each segment from world space onto the screen. Far segments are small and near the horizon; near segments are large at the bottom. Add per-segment curve and hill values and you get a road that bends and rolls. In this lesson you build that segmented pseudo-3D renderer with projection, curves, hills, and AI traffic.

Projection: the core formula

Each segment has a world position (x, y, z) relative to the camera. Project it to screen with a simple perspective divide. The camera sits at cameraZ behind the player with a height above the road.

project.cpp
struct Projected { float sx, sy, sw; float scale; }; Projected project(float wx, float wy, float wz, float camX, float camY, float camZ, float camDepth, int W, int H, float roadW) { float dz = wz - camZ; if (dz < 0.1f) dz = 0.1f; Projected p; p.scale = camDepth / dz; // closer = bigger p.sx = (W/2) + (p.scale * (wx - camX) * W/2); p.sy = (H/2) - (p.scale * (wy - camY) * H/2); p.sw = (p.scale * roadW * W/2); return p; }

camDepth is derived from field of view: camDepth = 1 / tan(fov/2). With fov around 100° you get a classic arcade look.

Drawing back-to-front

Render from the farthest visible segment toward the camera, drawing each as a trapezoid (a quad) between the previous projected point and this one. Alternate the rumble-strip and grass colors per segment band for the scrolling-stripes illusion.

render.cpp
int baseSeg = (int)(position / SEG_LEN); float x = 0, dx = 0; // accumulated curve offset float maxY = H; for (int n = 0; n < DRAW_DIST; n++) { Segment& s = road[(baseSeg + n) % road.size()]; float camZ = position - (segWrapped ? track : 0); Projected p = project(playerX*roadW - x, s.y, (baseSeg+n)*SEG_LEN, 0, camH, camZ, camDepth, W, H, roadW); x += dx; dx += s.curve; // curve bends the road if (p.sy >= maxY) continue; // hidden behind a hill maxY = p.sy; Color road = ((baseSeg+n)/3) % 2 ? DARKGRAY : GRAY; drawTrapezoid(prev, p, road); // quad from prev slice to this prev = p; }
Tip.
The hill effect is automatic: hills change each segment's world y, which changes sy. The if (p.sy >= maxY) continue; line is what hides road that dips behind a crest.

Curves and steering

The player's horizontal road position playerX (in road-half units, -1 to 1) changes with steering. Critically, fast cornering pushes you outward — apply centrifugal drift proportional to the current segment's curve and your speed.

drive.cpp
float speedPct = speed / MAX_SPEED; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_RIGHT)) playerX += 0.02f * speedPct; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_LEFT)) playerX -= 0.02f * speedPct; playerX -= curSeg.curve * speedPct * CENTRIFUGAL; // pushed out of turns if (IsKeyDown(KEY_UP)) speed += ACCEL * dt; else speed -= DECEL * dt; if (fabsf(playerX) > 1.0f) speed -= OFFROAD_DRAG * dt; // grass slows you speed = Clamp(speed, 0, MAX_SPEED); position += speed * dt;

AI traffic as sprites

Other cars are billboards placed at a road position and Z. Project them like road geometry using their segment's scale, and draw bigger as they approach. Cull anything behind the camera or beyond the draw distance.

traffic.cpp
for (Car& c : traffic) { c.z += c.speed * dt; float dz = c.z - position; if (dz < 0 || dz > DRAW_DIST*SEG_LEN) continue; float scale = camDepth / dz; float sx = W/2 + scale * (c.offset*roadW - playerX*roadW) * W/2; float sw = scale * CAR_W * W/2; DrawTextureEx(carTex, { sx - sw/2, H/2 - sw }, 0, sw/carTex.width, WHITE); }
Law of pseudo-3D.
Everything is projection plus painter's order. Sort and draw far-to-front, accumulate curve as you walk segments, and let hills emerge from the rising-floor occlusion test.
🎮 Build This: Racing (Pseudo-3D) — Coastline

This STARTS a new genre: the OutRun-style arcade racer. No prior game extends into it; it is a fresh rendering technique distinct from p4-14's raycasting.

  1. Build a track as a vector of segments, each with a curve and y (hill) value; add straights, gentle and sharp curves, and rolling hills.
  2. Implement the projection function and draw the road back-to-front as trapezoids with alternating colors.
  3. Add the rising-floor occlusion test so hills hide road behind crests.
  4. Add steering, acceleration, centrifugal drift in turns, and off-road slowdown on grass.
  5. Place AI traffic cars as billboards projected by depth; cull off-screen ones.
  6. Add a speedometer and a lap timer, and make the track loop seamlessly.

title: A Third-Person / Over-the-Shoulder Shooter
html:
The over-the-shoulder feel in 2D

A third-person shooter puts the camera behind and offset from the character, with an aim reticle the player can place independently. We will build this top-down with an over-the-shoulder camera offset: the view biases toward where you aim, the reticle floats ahead of the player, enemies use line-of-sight to decide when to fire, and cover blocks both bullets and sight. This combines the twin-stick controls (p4-13) with smarter cameras and AI.

Reticle offset and an aim-biased camera

The reticle sits between the player and the mouse, not on top of either. The camera target leans from the player toward the reticle so you can see more of what you are aiming at — the signature over-the-shoulder framing.

aimcam.cpp
Vector2 mouseW = GetScreenToWorld2D(GetMousePosition(), cam); Vector2 aimDir = Vector2Normalize(Vector2Subtract(mouseW, player.pos)); // reticle floats a fixed distance ahead, capped to the mouse Vector2 reticle = Vector2Add(player.pos, Vector2Scale(aimDir, RETICLE_DIST)); // camera target leans toward the aim (the shoulder offset) Vector2 bias = Vector2Scale(aimDir, CAM_LEAN); // e.g. 90 px Vector2 goal = Vector2Add(player.pos, bias); cam.target = Vector2Lerp(cam.target, goal, 1.0f - expf(-6.0f*dt));
Tip.
Keep CAM_LEAN modest. Too much and the player drifts off-screen; the camera should hint at the aim direction, not abandon the character.

Line-of-sight with a segment-vs-box test

An enemy should only see and shoot the player if no cover blocks the straight line between them. Test that line segment against every cover rectangle; if any blocks it, there is no line of sight.

los.cpp
bool segHitsRect(Vector2 a, Vector2 b, Rectangle r) { Vector2 p; return CheckCollisionLines(a,b,{r.x,r.y},{r.x+r.width,r.y},&p) || CheckCollisionLines(a,b,{r.x+r.width,r.y},{r.x+r.width,r.y+r.height},&p) || CheckCollisionLines(a,b,{r.x+r.width,r.y+r.height},{r.x,r.y+r.height},&p) || CheckCollisionLines(a,b,{r.x,r.y+r.height},{r.x,r.y},&p); } bool hasLOS(Vector2 from, Vector2 to) { for (const Rectangle& c : covers) if (segHitsRect(from, to, c)) return false; return true; }

Enemy AI: a tiny state machine

Each enemy runs a three-state FSM: idle until it gets line of sight, then chase to close distance, then fire when in range and visible. Losing sight returns it to chase, then idle after a timeout.

enemy.cpp
enum St { IDLE, CHASE, FIRE }; bool sees = hasLOS(e.pos, player.pos) && Vector2Distance(e.pos, player.pos) < SIGHT_RANGE; switch (e.state) { case IDLE: if (sees) e.state = CHASE; break; case CHASE: { if (!sees) { e.state = IDLE; break; } Vector2 d = Vector2Normalize(Vector2Subtract(player.pos, e.pos)); e.pos = Vector2Add(e.pos, Vector2Scale(d, ENEMY_SPD*dt)); if (Vector2Distance(e.pos, player.pos) < FIRE_RANGE) e.state = FIRE; break; } case FIRE: if (!sees) { e.state = CHASE; break; } if ((e.shootCd -= dt) <= 0) { fireEnemyBullet(e.pos, player.pos); e.shootCd = 0.8f; } break; }
Tip.
Reuse the bullet pool and particle system from p4-13 directly. Enemy bullets and player bullets share the same update; only the owner flag differs for collision.

Cover that matters

Cover rectangles block bullets too: when stepping a bullet, kill it if it enters any cover. Combined with the LOS test, this creates real tactical play — the player ducks behind a crate, breaking sight, and the enemy stops firing and advances to flank.

coverhit.cpp
for (Bullet& b : pool) if (b.alive) for (const Rectangle& c : covers) if (CheckCollisionPointRec(b.pos, c)) { b.alive = false; emit(b.pos, 6, 120, GRAY); }
Law of cover shooters.
Line-of-sight is the soul of the genre. When sight and bullets obey the same geometry, players intuitively understand cover without a tutorial.
🎮 Build This: Third-Person Shooter — Breakpoint

This STARTS a new genre: the over-the-shoulder / top-down third-person shooter. It directly EXTENDS the twin-stick shooter (p4-13), reusing its bullet pool and particle system.

  1. Set up WASD movement plus a floating reticle that sits ahead of the player toward the mouse.
  2. Add an over-the-shoulder camera that leans its target toward the aim direction with smoothing.
  3. Place cover rectangles and implement the segment-vs-rect line-of-sight test.
  4. Give enemies an idle/chase/fire FSM gated on line-of-sight and range.
  5. Make bullets (player and enemy) die on cover and emit impact particles.
  6. Verify that ducking behind cover breaks enemy fire and that they reposition to regain sight.
